title: First backflip on ground
description: Months of progressions — finally landed it on grass.
article: I wanted to learn a backflip for no practical reason other than it seemed impossible and therefore worth doing. Started with rolls, then assisted flips into a foam pit, then hundreds of attempts on grass where the landing actually hurts.
The breakthrough wasn't courage — it was timing. I kept opening up too early. Once I learned to commit and tuck harder off the ground, it clicked in an afternoon.
Still not clean. Still scary every time. But that's the point — proof that showing up and doing the reps on hard things transfers everywhere else.

title: Will code HTML for t-shirts
description: What consistent lifting actually looks like.
article: I started lifting at 15 with no plan, just a cheap gym membership and YouTube. Early progress was fast because anything works when you've never trained.

The real work started when progress slowed. Tracking every session, fixing sleep, eating enough protein even when I didn't want to. The photo is just a snapshot — the story is three years of not skipping leg day.
I don't lift for aesthetics alone. It's the one domain where effort maps cleanly to outcome. That clarity keeps me sane when code problems are ambiguous for weeks.

title: Running for long
description: Running data from Strava — the boring work that compounds.
article: I used to hate cardio. Treating runs like lifting sessions — progressive overload, tracked pace, deliberate recovery — made it click.
Strava turned it into a game. Segments, weekly volume, watching aerobic base build over months. None of it is impressive compared to real runners. The point is I do it anyway.
Cardio is the discipline I least enjoy and most need. It keeps energy high for long coding sessions and stops me from becoming a person who only trains mirror muscles.

title: Polish Tatry Mountains
description: Hiking in the mountains — training that doesn't feel like training.
article: Poland has solid mountains close enough for a weekend. I go whenever I need to reset — long elevation, cold air, no screens.
Hiking is the anti-gym. No reps counted, no PRs, just hours of moving uphill until your legs burn and your head clears. It's the only workout I do purely for how it feels during, not after.
The mountains remind me that progress isn't always measurable. Sometimes it's just being somewhere hard to reach and coming back down tired and calm.
